citation: Bailey AM, McMurry TL, Cormier JM, Funk JR, Crandall JR, Mack CD, Myers BS, Arbogast KB. Comparison of Laboratory and On-Field Performance of American Football Helmets. Ann Biomed Eng. 2020. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10439-020-02627-5
abstract: This study assessed the relationship between laboratory performance scores and on-field concussion rates for 31 football helmet models worn by NFL players during the 2015-2019 seasons. Linear impactor tests at six impact locations and three velocities were used to compute a Helmet Performance Score (HPS) based on head injury criterion (HIC) and diffuse axonal multi-axis evaluation (DAMAGE) metrics. On-field concussion data were collected across over 5.4 million player-plays, and logistic regression related HPS to helmet-specific concussion rates while controlling for position, play type, season year, season type, and prior concussion history. Helmets prohibited by the NFL/NFLPA based on poor laboratory performance had significantly higher on-field concussion risk.
tldr: Laboratory helmet performance scores positively correlate with on-field concussion rates in the NFL (r² = 0.61, p < 0.001), meaning helmets that perform worse in lab testing are associated with higher concussion rates on the field. Helmets prohibited by the NFL/NFLPA based on poor lab performance had 24% higher odds of concussion (OR 1.24; 95% CI 1.04-1.47; p = 0.017) compared to non-prohibited models. Football equipment staff should use annual NFL/NFLPA helmet ratings as the primary guide for equipment procurement and ensure all players are in approved, high-performing helmets. Kickoff play types carry the highest concussion probability, and prior concussion history is a strong independent predictor of future concussion risk.
methods: Cross-sectional observational study. Two samples each of 31 helmet models worn by NFL players (2015-2019) were tested using a pneumatic ram and sliding table at 18 test conditions (3 speeds x 6 locations). Six-degree-of-freedom head kinematics computed HARM (0.0148 x HIC + 15.6 x DAMAGE) per condition; a weighted composite HPS was calculated per helmet. NFL Game Statistics and Information System and Helmet Tracker RFID data identified helmet use across 5,460,388 player-plays. Concussion diagnoses came from the NFL electronic medical record. Logistic mixed regression with random player effects estimated concussion probability, controlling for position, play type, season year, season type, and prior concussion count. Helmets were also grouped as prohibited or non-prohibited per the 2020 NFL/NFLPA helmet poster.
findings: HPS for the 31 helmets ranged from 0.874 to 1.163 (mean 1.03, SD 0.07); 24 of 31 helmets were within one SD of the mean. A positive correlation between HPS and on-field concussion rate was found for helmets with >50,000 player-plays (r² = 0.61, p < 0.001). On-field concussion rates ranged from 1.23 to 3.11 per 10,000 player-plays. Prohibited helmets had a raw concussion rate 1.62x higher than non-prohibited helmets and significantly higher adjusted odds (OR 1.24; 95% CI 1.04-1.47; p = 0.017). Returned kickoffs had the highest concussion probability; tight ends and wide receivers had the highest positional risk. Concussion probability increased significantly with each additional prior concussion (p < 0.001). Concussion probability was significantly lower in 2018 and 2019 relative to 2015.
limitations: Laboratory testing used only helmet-to-helmet impact conditions accounting for approximately 36% of NFL concussions, not helmet-to-ground or helmet-to-shoulder impacts. Only one helmet size per model was tested; padding thickness varies with size within a model. Player position served as a surrogate for impact frequency and severity rather than direct head impact sensor data. Findings are specific to professional-level football and may not apply to youth, high school, or collegiate play.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football equipment staff should consult annual NFL/NFLPA helmet laboratory testing results and ensure all players use models not on the prohibited list. Equipment upgrade programs targeting high-risk positions (tight ends, wide receivers, cornerbacks) offer the greatest potential for reducing concussion incidence. Kickoff rule changes already implemented in 2016 and 2018 appear to have contributed to declining concussion rates. Prior concussion history must be carefully tracked as an independent risk factor when making return-to-play and medical clearance decisions.
athleteDev: Equipment selection is a foundational element of player development programs. Integrating up-to-date helmet performance ratings into equipment purchasing decisions protects athlete longevity and developmental continuity. Educating players from high school through the NFL about helmet selection may reduce cumulative head impact burden across a career and support sustained physical development.
rtp: Prior concussion count was the strongest modifiable predictor of future concussion risk, underscoring the importance of thorough concussion documentation and conservative return-to-play decision-making. Players returning from concussion who wear lower-performing helmet models should be considered for equipment upgrades. Preseason play had the highest odds ratio for concussion relative to the regular season, warranting heightened RTP protocol vigilance during preseason camp.

citation: Merrigan JJ, Rentz LE, Hornsby WG, Wagle JP, Stone JD, Smith HT, Galster SM, Joseph M, Hagen JA. Comparisons of Countermovement Jump Force-Time Characteristics Among NCAA Division I American Football Athletes: Use of Principal Component Analysis. J Strength Cond Res. 2021;XX(X):000-000.
abstract: This cross-sectional study examined countermovement jump (CMJ) force-time characteristics across four positional groups (skills, hybrid, linemen, specialists) in 82 NCAA Division I Power 5 football athletes using dual force plates. One-way ANOVA evaluated positional differences in absolute and relative metrics, and principal component analysis (PCA) was used to reduce the dimensionality of 27 force-time variables. Four principal components explained 89.5% of the variance in CMJ metrics, representing explosive transferability to concentric power, powerful eccentric loading, countermovement strategy, and jump height and power output. Linemen produced the greatest absolute forces but had the lowest jump heights, while skill players achieved the highest jump heights and best relative power outputs.
tldr: Force plate CMJ testing reveals meaningful positional differences in jump strategy that go well beyond jump height alone. Skill players jump highest (46.7 cm) with superior relative power, while linemen produce the greatest absolute forces but struggle to transfer eccentric energy into concentric power efficiently, reflected in longer concentric phase durations and lower RSImod. Four principal components explained 89.5% of CMJ variance organized around: (1) explosive eccentric-to-concentric transferability (RSImod, concentric mean power), (2) eccentric loading quality, (3) countermovement strategy depth and duration, and (4) jump height and power output. Practitioners should monitor 1-2 metrics from each PC dimension rather than relying on a single outcome measure, and should design position-specific programs emphasizing elastic properties and stretch-shortening cycle development for linemen, and maximal strength development for skills and hybrids.
methods: Cross-sectional design using off-season (January) CMJ testing of 82 NCAA Division I football athletes (ages 18-22) grouped as linemen (n=21), hybrid (n=18), skill (n=30), and specialists (n=13). Athletes performed 2 maximal-effort, no arm-swing CMJs on ForceDecks FD4000 dual force platforms sampled at 1,000 Hz after a standardized warm-up. Metrics were averaged across two trials. One-way ANOVA with post hoc Tukey tests evaluated positional differences (p < 0.05). PCA using the correlational method via the FactoMineR package in R 4.0.3 reduced 27 force-time metrics. Bartlett's sphericity test and Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin sampling adequacy (KMO = 0.80, meritorious) confirmed factor analysis suitability. Scree plot and eigenvalue >1.0 criterion identified four principal components. Squared cosine (cos²) values and v-test statistics quantified variable importance and positional group loadings.
findings: Linemen (132.0 ± 14.5 kg) had significantly greater body mass than all groups (p < 0.001) and produced greater absolute eccentric and concentric forces, but lower relative forces and power. Skill players achieved the highest jump height (46.7 ± 4.6 cm) vs hybrid (42.8 ± 5.5), specialists (38.7 ± 4.0), and linemen (34.1 ± 5.3 cm). Skills and hybrids had the highest RSImod values (0.61 and 0.57 m/s respectively) vs linemen (0.41 m/s). Four PCs explained 89.5% of CMJ variance (PC1 = 52.3%, PC2 = 22.2%, PC3 = 9.2%, PC4 = 5.8%). PC1 was driven by RSImod (cos² = 0.85), concentric mean power (0.84), concentric RPD 50ms (0.83), concentric RPD 100ms (0.83), and eccentric-to-concentric mean force ratio (0.82). Linemen had a significant negative loading on PC1 (v-test = -5.6); skill players had significant positive loadings on PC1 (v-test = 4.4) and PC2 (v-test = 2.4). Concentric phase duration was significantly longer in linemen than skill and hybrid athletes.
limitations: Single time point (off-season January) limits generalizability across training phases and longitudinal metric tracking. Body composition and muscle physiology data were not collected, which would provide important context. PCA metric combinations may change with different scenarios, timepoints, competition levels, or sports. Sample was from one Division I Power 5 program, limiting external validity. Reliability of rate of force development metrics must be considered when applying these findings.
practicalImplications: Strength and conditioning staff at NFL and college programs should use force plate CMJ testing by position rather than relying solely on jump height. Linemen programs should emphasize exercises that improve stretch-shortening cycle efficiency and elastic energy storage at the hip, knee, and ankle to improve eccentric-to-concentric power transfer. Skill and hybrid programs should focus on maximizing absolute strength as the primary ceiling on total relative power output. For efficient monitoring, select 1-2 metrics per PCA dimension: RSImod or concentric mean power (PC1), eccentric peak velocity or peak power (PC2), countermovement depth or braking duration (PC3), and jump height (PC4).
athleteDev: PCA-guided metric selection enables efficient, position-specific neuromuscular monitoring throughout training blocks. Off-season CMJ profiling can identify athletes with poor eccentric-to-concentric transfer—especially linemen—who may benefit from targeted plyometric and reactive strength training blocks. Jump strategy profiles can inform appropriate positional assignment and individualized programming decisions for developing athletes at the collegiate and professional levels.
rtp: CMJ force-time monitoring provides a sensitive, non-fatiguing neuromuscular assessment tool for tracking recovery and readiness following injury. Tracking RSImod and concentric mean power longitudinally can help identify when an athlete has returned to baseline neuromuscular output. Position-specific normative reference values derived from this type of data can improve RTP decision-making by establishing meaningful benchmarks for determining readiness to return to full-contact practice.

citation: Roach MA, Owens MF. Competitive Position, Expectations, and Managerial Approaches to Risk in National Football League Football. J Sport Manag. 2025;39(5):342-353. https://doi.org/10.1123/jsm.2024-0254
abstract: This study analyzed how NFL coaches incorporate competitive position and win expectations into fourth-down decision-making using play-by-play data from 2008-2018 NFL regular seasons. Probit regression models estimated the likelihood of attempting a fourth-down conversion as a function of current win probability, pregame betting line, and maximum in-game win probability, while controlling for game situation variables, coach experience, and season fixed effects. The study tested predictions from prospect theory and rank-order tournament theory, finding that teams in weaker competitive positions and those with higher prior in-game success were systematically more aggressive on fourth down.
tldr: NFL coaches take more risk on fourth down when their win probability is low and when they have experienced high in-game win probabilities earlier in the game, consistent with prospect theory loss-frame behavior. Despite this increased aggressiveness, teams still systematically attempt too few fourth-down plays relative to the win-probability-maximizing threshold, representing a persistent coaching inefficiency. Being a pregame betting favorite and having a high prior in-game max win probability both independently increase fourth-down aggressiveness. Football operations and analytics staff should use expected-value frameworks to guide fourth-down decisions, as increased aggressiveness in these situations—even in non-optimal subsets—improves expected win outcomes by approximately 1.56 percentage points per attempt.
methods: Play-by-play data from 2008-2018 NFL regular seasons obtained from sports-reference.com (37,692 total regular season fourth downs; primary analytical subset of 18,366 plays where attempting a conversion offered a greater expected change in win probability than punting or a field goal). Three expected-change-in-win-probability models estimated the optimal fourth-down decision for each play. Probit regression models estimated probability of a fourth-down attempt as a function of win probability, betting line, max win probability, and their interactions, controlling for field position, yards to go, game quarter, lead, home team, division rival, neutral site, game week, and coach experience. OLS regression estimated actual change in win probability from fourth-down attempts.
findings: Teams attempted fourth-down conversions 12.2% of the time in the primary subset, converting 64.6% of attempts. A 10-percentage-point increase in win probability reduced the likelihood of attempting a fourth down by 2.67 percentage points in the optimal-play subset (Specification 4). Being favored by one additional betting-line point increased fourth-down attempt likelihood by approximately 0.26 percentage points. A 10-percentage-point increase in maximum in-game win probability increased the likelihood of attempting a fourth-down play by 0.54 percentage points in the optimal subset. Teams in the 0-25% win probability bin attempted plays 18.2% of the time vs 8.2% for teams in the 75-100% bin. Fourth-down attempts generated an average expected change in win probability of +1.56 percentage points relative to punting or a field goal across all situations, confirming persistent under-aggressiveness. Interactions between win probability and betting line, and win probability and max win probability, confirmed that reference point effects are concentrated in low win probability situations.
limitations: Win probability data from sports-reference.com were unavailable after 2018, limiting the sample to a period before the wider adoption of analytics-driven fourth-down culture. The study cannot separate rational quality-differential explanations from behavioral loss-aversion explanations for the betting-line effects. Coach-level motivations and individual decision processes are unobservable. Reference points are theoretical constructs not directly measured. Results may reflect changing league norms during the study period.
practicalImplications: Football operations and coaching staffs should implement data-driven fourth-down decision frameworks that use expected win probability as the primary decision criterion. The persistent tendency to punt or kick when analytics favor going for it represents a systematic and exploitable inefficiency. Understanding that teams in desperate positions are more aggressive on fourth down can inform defensive preparation and opponent game-planning. Organizations should track their own fourth-down decision accuracy over time to identify systematic biases in their coaching staff.
athleteDev: While primarily a sport management and economics paper, findings have indirect implications for game planning and situational football preparation. Understanding that fourth-down conversion attempts succeed 64.6% of the time in optimal situations informs offensive and defensive coordinators about practice priorities in short-yardage, goal-line, and fourth-down scenarios. Investing developmental resources in these situational packages may yield compounding win-probability benefits.
rtp: This paper does not address return-to-play topics. Its focus is on in-game managerial risk decisions and coaching behavior, not player health or injury recovery.

citation: Blake AL, McVicar CL, Retino M, Hall EE, Ketcham CJ. Concussion history influences sleep disturbances, symptoms, and quality of life in collegiate student-athletes. Sleep Health. 2019;5(1):72-77. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.sleh.2018.10.011
abstract: This cross-sectional study determined whether self-reported concussion history was associated with persistent sleep disturbances, concussion symptoms, and quality of life (QOL) dysfunctions in 82 collegiate club and varsity student-athletes not currently recovering from a concussion. Participants completed an online survey including concussion history categorized as 0, 1-2, or 3+ concussions, the Rivermead Post-Concussion Symptom Questionnaire (RPQ), and an adapted Rivermead Head Injury Follow-Up Questionnaire (RHFUQ). Athletes with prior concussions reported significantly more sleep disturbances, concussion symptoms, and QOL dysfunctions than those with no concussion history, even after returning to sport and academics.
tldr: Collegiate athletes with a history of concussion continue to experience significantly more sleep disturbances, ongoing concussion symptoms, and reduced quality of life even after returning to play and academics. The number of sleep disturbances strongly correlated with both symptom severity (r = 0.48) and QOL dysfunction (r = 0.35). For football staff and athletic trainers, these findings emphasize that post-concussion monitoring must extend well beyond initial symptom clearance and RTP milestones—athletes with multiple concussions may require ongoing sleep screening and multidisciplinary support to protect academic performance, athletic output, and mental health.
methods: Cross-sectional online survey of 82 collegiate student-athletes (club and varsity; ages 18-25, mean 20.3 ± 1.5 years) from a small private liberal arts university in the southeastern United States, not currently recovering from a concussion. Concussion history was self-reported as 0, 1-2, or 3+ concussions. Sleep disturbances were measured using a checklist of 6 common disturbances (difficulty falling asleep, restlessness, sleeping too much, sleeping too little, difficulty staying asleep, fatigue). Concussion symptoms were assessed with the 16-item RPQ (5-point Likert scale, max score 64). QOL was assessed with an adapted 10-item RHFUQ (5-point Likert scale, max score 40). One-way ANOVAs with LSD post hoc tests compared concussion history groups (alpha < 0.05). Pearson correlations assessed relationships between sleep disturbances, symptoms, and QOL.
findings: The most common sleep disturbances were fatigue (32%), sleeping too little (27%), restlessness (24%), and difficulty falling asleep (24%). Significant group differences were found in total sleep disturbances (F = 3.73, P = .028), with the 0-concussion group having significantly fewer than the 3+ group (P = .012). Significant differences existed in total symptoms (F = 3.72, P = .029) and symptom severity (F = 4.51, P = .014), with the 0-concussion group reporting fewer symptoms than both 1-2 (P = .020) and 3+ (P = .046) concussion groups. Significant differences were found in QOL dysfunctions (F = 6.20, P = .003) and QOL dysfunction severity (F = 4.34, P = .016), with the 0-concussion group reporting fewer than both concussion groups (P = .005 and P = .006). Sleep disturbances positively correlated with symptoms (r = .54, P < .001), symptom severity (r = .48, P < .001), QOL dysfunctions (r = .35, P = .001), and QOL severity (r = .34, P = .002). Sport level (varsity vs. club) did not influence outcomes.
limitations: Modest sample size (N = 82) from a single institution limits generalizability. Cross-sectional design prevents causal inference and time since last concussion varied across participants. Self-reported concussion history is subject to recall bias. Sport-specific differences could not be fully evaluated due to small within-sport sample sizes. No objective sleep measures (e.g., actigraphy) were used. The RHFUQ was adapted from its original format, which may affect validity.
practicalImplications: Football medical and performance staff should implement routine sleep and symptom screening for athletes with any concussion history, not only during the acute recovery window. Athletes with 3 or more concussions are particularly vulnerable to persistent sleep disturbances that impair recovery, cognitive function, and academic performance. Integrating sleep quality assessments into annual pre-participation physicals and ongoing wellness monitoring programs can help identify athletes who may benefit from referral for sleep interventions, neurological follow-up, or performance support services.
athleteDev: Persistent sleep disturbances after concussion impair muscle recovery, immune function, cognitive processing, attention, and motivation—all of which are critical for athlete development. Football programs should address sleep hygiene as a performance variable for any athlete with a concussion history, potentially integrating wearable sleep monitoring and structured sleep education as components of the development program, particularly for athletes with 3 or more prior concussions.
rtp: This study demonstrates that concussion symptoms and QOL dysfunctions persist even after formal return-to-play, indicating that current RTP protocols may not fully capture lingering recovery deficits. Clinicians should consider extending post-clearance follow-up to include validated sleep assessments, especially in athletes with multiple concussion histories. Athletes reporting ongoing sleep disturbances after neurological clearance may warrant re-evaluation before resuming full-contact practice or high-demand training.

citation: Kerr ZY, Register-Mihalik JK, Kay MC, DeFreese JD, Marshall SW, Guskiewicz KM. Concussion Nondisclosure During Professional Career Among a Cohort of Former National Football League Athletes. Am J Sports Med. 2017. DOI: 10.1177/0363546517728264
abstract: This retrospective cross-sectional study examined the prevalence of and factors associated with concussion nondisclosure to team medical staff among 829 former NFL players who played from pre-WWII through 2001, using data from the Retired NFL Players General Health Survey administered in 2001 and 2010. Among those who recalled sustaining concussions, 50.3% reported not disclosing at least one concussion to medical staff during their professional career. Multivariable binomial regression identified race/ethnicity, position, playing era, and career concussion history as significant factors associated with nondisclosure, with nonwhite players and those with more career concussions showing the highest rates.
tldr: Half of former NFL players who recalled sustaining concussions during their professional career (50.3%) reported not disclosing at least one concussion to team medical staff. Nondisclosure was more prevalent among nonwhite players (adjusted PR = 1.19), linebackers and linemen, those who played after the 1976 spearing rule change, and those with higher career concussion totals—ranging from 35.5% among players with 1-2 career concussions to 75.0% among those with 10 or more. For current NFL and college football programs, these findings reinforce that concussion detection cannot rely on athlete self-disclosure alone; proactive sideline surveillance, independent neurotrauma consultants, racially and culturally concordant medical staff, and a team culture that removes perceived penalties for disclosure are essential for meaningful concussion management.
methods: Retrospective cross-sectional survey using the Retired NFL Players General Health Survey (GHS). The baseline 2001 GHS was sent to all living NFL Retired Players Association members (n = 3,729; 68.0% response rate). A 2010 follow-up GHS was sent to 2,102 eligible participants (62.6% response). The final analytical sample was 829 players who reported at least one career concussion with complete disclosure data. Concussion was defined as a blow to the head followed by associated symptoms. Multivariable binomial regression with Poisson residuals and robust variance estimation estimated adjusted prevalence ratios (PR) with 95% confidence intervals, controlling for race/ethnicity, number of years played, primary position played, professional career concussion history, and playing era (pre vs. post-1976 spearing rule change). Interaction terms between concussion history and playing era were included given P < .10 threshold.
findings: Overall, 417 (50.3%) of 829 former players reported not disclosing at least one concussion during their professional career. Nonwhite players had a significantly higher adjusted prevalence of nondisclosure than white/non-Hispanic players (PR = 1.19; 95% CI 1.02-1.38). In multivariable models, linebackers (PR = 1.53; 95% CI 1.11-2.11), offensive linemen (PR = 1.46; 95% CI 1.06-2.00), and defensive linemen (PR = 1.48; 95% CI 1.04-2.08) had significantly higher nondisclosure than running backs. A significant interaction between playing era and concussion history was found (P = .08): among players with 1-2 career concussions, post-spearing-rule-change players had 41% higher nondisclosure prevalence (PR = 1.41; 95% CI 1.05-1.89); among those with 3-4 concussions, 37% higher (PR = 1.37; 95% CI 1.07-1.74). Nondisclosure prevalence ranged from 35.5% (1-2 career concussions) to 75.0% (10+ career concussions).
limitations: Retrospective self-report design is subject to recall bias, social desirability bias, and measurement error inherent to reports of nonreports. The cohort does not include players who retired after 2001, limiting generalizability to modern NFL athletes. Reasons for nondisclosure were not captured. Findings may not generalize to other competition levels or sports. Concussion diagnoses relied on participant recall and may not meet clinical definitions. The study cannot establish causation.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football programs must recognize that concussion nondisclosure is systemic and cannot be solved by athlete education alone. Proactive concussion identification strategies—including independent sideline neurotrauma consultants, video review protocols, and structured symptom check-ins—are essential. Programs should evaluate racial and cultural concordance between athletes and medical staff, as discordance may reduce disclosure comfort. Team culture must explicitly remove perceived career penalties for disclosure. Athletes with high career concussion histories require enhanced active surveillance beyond standard self-report mechanisms.
athleteDev: Nondisclosure of concussions during developmental years compounds long-term brain health risk, as unmanaged concussions are associated with depression, cognitive impairment, and chronic traumatic encephalopathy. Programs must build a culture of psychological safety around concussion reporting from the earliest developmental stages—youth through collegiate and professional levels—emphasizing that disclosure protects career longevity rather than threatening it. Trust between athletes and medical staff is a prerequisite for effective concussion management.
rtp: Because nondisclosure is highest among players with the most career concussions, current RTP protocols that rely heavily on athlete self-report of symptoms are structurally vulnerable. Active surveillance tools including validated sideline assessment instruments, independent neurotrauma consultants, and video review—as currently employed in the NFL—are essential complements to self-report. RTP protocols should be designed with the explicit assumption that some proportion of concussed players will not voluntarily disclose, particularly among linemen and linebackers who show the highest nondisclosure rates.
